items.title: Getting Started with Docker: A Beginner’s Guide
items.author: Pravesh Sudha
items.content:
Welcome to the world of DevOps! Today, we’re diving into an exciting tool that solves the classic software engineering problem:

“It worked on my machine, but not on yours!”

Yep, we’ve all been there. But thanks to , this headache is now a thing of the past!

In this blog, we’ll explore how Docker has revolutionized the DevOps industry, saving companies in computing costs. We’ll also get hands-on by creating a for a cloud-native monitoring application that tracks .

So, grab your coffee, and let’s get started! 🚀

💡 What is Docker? Understanding Containers & Why They Matter

Docker is a platform that allows us to develop, ship, and run applications in containers.

Wait… containers? Shipping? What’s going on here? 🚢Let’s slow down and break it down step by step.

Think of Docker as a tool that helps us create a of our application. Using this blueprint, we can run the application inside a .

But what exactly is a in Docker? 🤔A is like a lightweight operating system that includes everything needed to run an application — its dependencies, libraries, and configurations.

Here’s the of using Docker:

  1. We create a (this is the exact name it should have).
  2. Inside the , we define how our application should be built and run.
  3. Using some , we generate a  — this is the actual of our application.
  4. Finally, we use the to run a , where our application will actually execute.

Why Use Docker? Isn’t This More Complicated?

At first, Docker might seem complex, but here’s why it’s :

🔹 :Before Docker, we used to run applications on the cloud. However, VMs often , leading to . With Docker, we use that run on VMs, making applications much faster and more efficient.

🔹 Consistency Across Environments:When we create a , we choose a for our project (like Ubuntu, Alpine, or Python). This ensures that all developers and servers use the exact same environment, preventing the “works on my machine but not yours”issue.

🔹 Multiple Applications on One Machine:With Docker, we can run multiple applications on the same VM. Before Docker, we could usually run only one application per VM. This means better and .

Now that we understand , let’s move forward and build something cool with it! 🚀

💡 Installing Docker & Running Your First Container

Enough theory — let’s jump into action! 🏃💨

Step 1: Installing Docker

First, we need to install Docker on our system. Here’s how:

✅ : Download from Docker’s official website. It provides an easy-to-use GUI along with the Docker CLI.

✅ : You can install Docker using terminal commands. If you’re on , run:

sudo apt update -ysudo apt install docker.io -y
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER && newgrp docker
docker --version  # This will display the installed Docker version.

Once installed, Docker is ready to roll! 🎉

Step 2: Understanding DockerHub & Image Management

Earlier, we talked about 📝 helping us create 📦. But what if we the image we created? Our would be gone!

To avoid this, we have  — a cloud-based repository where we can store and share Docker images, just like .

  • : Open for everyone to use.
  • : Only accessible to authorized users.

This allows teams to collaborate and deploy applications consistently across different environments.

Step 3: Running Your First Docker Container

Let’s test Docker by running a :

docker run -d -p 8080:80 docker/welcome-to-docker
  • docker run → Runs a new container.
  • -d → Runs the container in (in the background).
  • -p 8080:80 → Maps on your local machine to inside the container.
  • docker/welcome-to-docker → The we are running, which is pre-configured to display a welcome message.

Now, open your web browser and go to:

You should see a Docker Congratulations page! 🎉

This page is served by the image, which contains a simple web server (NGINX or Apache)displaying the welcome message.

Now that you’ve run your first Docker container, let’s move on to  🚀

💡 Building Our Own Docker Image: Cloud Monitoring App

Now that we’ve experimented with a demo Docker container, it’s time to build ! 🚀

We’ll use a Flask-based cloud monitoring application that displays of the machine it runs on.

Step 1: Cloning the Project Repository

First, let’s clone the project from GitHub:

git clone https://github.com/Pravesh-Sudha/cloud-native-monitoring-app.git

Next, navigate inside the project directory:

cd cloud-native-monitoring-app

Inside this directory, you’ll find a . Let’s break it down!

Step 2: Understanding the Dockerfile

Here’s the for our Flask-based cloud monitoring app:

# 1️⃣ Set the base imageFROM python:3.9-slim-buster  
# 2️⃣ Set the working directory inside the containerWORKDIR /app  
# 3️⃣ Copy the dependency file into the containerCOPY requirements.txt .  
# 4️⃣ Install dependenciesRUN pip3 install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt  
# 5️⃣ Copy the entire project into the containerCOPY . .  
# 6️⃣ Set environment variablesENV FLASK_RUN_HOST=0.0.0.0  
# 7️⃣ Expose the application’s portEXPOSE 5000  
# 8️⃣ Define the command to run the Flask appCMD ["flask", "run"]

📌 FROM python:3.9-slim-buster → This sets our .

  • We’re using python:3.9-slim-buster, a version of Python 3.9.
  • Slim images are in size, making the container more efficient.

📌 WORKDIR /app → This sets the inside the container.

  • Just like we create a folder for our project, we define /app as the directory where our application code will live inside the container.

📌 COPY requirements.txt . → This copies requirements.txt into the container.

  • This file contains all the required for our Flask app.

📌 RUN pip3 install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt → This installs the required .

📌 COPY . . → This copies our application files into the container.

📌 ENV FLASK_RUN_HOST=0.0.0.0 → This sets .

  • Here, we define FLASK_RUN_HOST=0.0.0.0 to allow our app to run properly inside a container or VM.

📌 EXPOSE 5000 → This exposes , the default port for Flask applications.

📌 CMD ["flask", "run"] → This defines the that will start our Flask app.

Step 3: Building the Docker Image

Now, let’s create a from this project. Run the following command:

docker build -t my-app:v1 .

🔹 -t my-app:v1 → Tags the image as with version v1.🔹 . → Tells Docker to use the as the build context.

Step 4: Running the Docker Container

Once the image is built successfully, let’s run it as a :

docker run -d -p 5000:5000 my-app:v1

🔹 -d → Runs the container in (in the background).🔹 -p 5000:5000 → Maps on your local machine to inside the container.

Now, open your browser and go to:

🎉 You should see the application running!

Each time you refresh the page, the CPU and memory usage metrics will .

Now that we’ve successfully built and run our Dockerized cloud monitoring app.

Congratulations! 🎉 You’ve just taken your first steps into the world of Docker by learning how it solves real-world software deployment problems, running a demo container, and even Dockerizing a Flask-based cloud monitoring application.

Here’s a quick recap of what we covered:

✅ A platform for building, shipping, and running applications in lightweight containers.✅ It solves compatibility issues, improves resource utilization, and allows multiple applications to run efficiently on the same machine.✅ We installed Docker, ran a sample container, created a , built a , and launched a for our project.

This is just the beginning! Docker is a widely used in DevOps, CI/CD pipelines, and cloud deployments. The next step? Try experimenting with , push your image to , or deploy your containerized app to ! 🚀

If you found this blog helpful, feel free to share it with your fellow DevOps enthusiasts! Have questions? Drop them in the comments — I’d love to help. 😊

items.title: Freeipa/RedHat IdM API
items.author: Aditya Jambhalikar
items.content:
Red Hat Identity Management (IdM) provides a centralised and unified way to manage identity stores, authentication, policies, and authorisation policies in a Linux-based domain.

It also provides an option to use API (IPA API) to perform all the tasks programatically.

There are lots of ways to use it, but let’s check 3 popular ways which are curl , python and ansible

When it comes to quick use of API, we always think of using cURL. To use it for freeipa api, we first need to obtain cookies (create session) by logging in and then use the cookies for any further operation

The first thing to do is to define the environmental variables of the username, password, and cookies file.

export COOKIEJAR="/tmp/ipa" # path to store the cookie fileexport _USERNAME="admin" # or any other user with appropriate permission for the task you want to perform
 export _PASSWORD="<admin_password>" 
# Notice the whitespace before export, 
# It will make sure that the entry is not added to the termianl command history

The above step is optional, you can otherwise just pass the values directly in the request.

The next step is to login and obtain the cookies

curl -k  \-H Referer:https://<ipa_fqdn>/ipa \
-H "Content-Type:application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
-H "Accept:text/plain" \
-c $COOKIEJAR -b $COOKIEJAR \
--data "user=$_USERNAME&password=$_PASSWORD" \https://<ipa_fqdn>/ipa/session/login_password

If this is successful, then you can send any request

e.g. Let’s fetch all the users present in freeipa

curl -k \    -H Referer:https://<ipa_fqdn>/ipa \
    -H "Content-Type:application/json" \
    -H "Accept:application/json"\
    -c $COOKIEJAR -b $COOKIEJAR \
    -d  '{"method": "user_find", "params": [[], {"all": true, "raw": true}]}' \    https://<ipa_fqdn>/ipa/session/json

The only part that will change in any request is the data-d

The Python way is similar as well. We create a session and then use it for any operation. There could be many ways to do it. But I prefer defining a class and using the session object.

import requestsclass IPA:
    def __init__(self, ipa_url):
        self.ipa_url = ipa_url
        self.session_url = f"https://{self.ipa_url}/ipa/session/json"
        self.referer = f"https://{self.ipa_url}/ipa"
        self.session = requests.Session()
        self.req_header = {"Referer": self.referer, 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 'Accept': 'application/json'}
    def login(self, username="admin", password="default_admin_password"):
        data = {"user": username, "password": password}
        login_url = f"https://{self.ipa_url}/ipa/session/login_password"
        header = {"Referer": self.referer, "Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded", "Accept": "text/plain"}
        res = self.session.post(login_url, headers=header, data=data, verify=False)
    def ipa_request(self, data):
        res = self.session.post(self.session_url, headers=self.req_header, data=json.dumps(data), verify=False)        data = {"method": "user_find", "params": [[], {"all": True, "sizelimit": 0}]}
        res = self.ipa_request(data)
        return res
    def group_add_member(self, group_cn, users):
        data = {"method": "group_add_member", "params": [[f"{group_cn}"],
                                                         {"all": True, "user": users}]}
        print(f"\nrequest to send is:\n{data}")
        res = self.ipa_request(data)
if __name__ == "__main__":
    ipa = IPA(ipa_url="ipa.local.com")    users = ipa.user_find() 
    all_users = users["result"]["result"]
    print(all_users)
    res = ipa.group_add_member("test_group", ["user1", "user2"])

The above code will print all users present in freeipa and add the users user1 and user2 to the user group test_group

Can you modify the code and add the function to create the users and user groups?

Ansible is the best when it comes to configuring and managing different kinds of environments. The community general provides a module to configure the freeipa objects. It doesn’t support everything, it has most of the useful configuration objects

# vim: ft=yaml---
- name: Freeipa configuration
  hosts: localhost  environment:
    IPA_PASS: "<admin_password>" # This can be env var or vault var
    IPA_HOST: ipa.local.com    - "/root/hostgroup_list.yaml"    - name: Add hostgroups
      community.general.ipa_hostgroup:
        name: "{{ item.name }}"
        description: "{{ item.description }}"
        validate_certs: False
      with_items: "{{ hostgroup_list }}"
      tags: hostgroups

/root/hostgroup_list.yaml

hostgroup_list:  - description: 'test_group'

After this, you can run the playbook

ansible-playbook /root/ipa_playbook.yaml

This was just a simple example, but there are many better ways to do it

e.g. creating an inventory files, group_vars, encrypting IPA_PASS with ansible-vault etc

The freeipa API makes things easier and reduces operational overhead

Freeipa/RedHat IdM API was originally published in DevOps.dev on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: gRPC: For High-Performance Microservices Communication
items.author: DAMAN
items.content:
gRPC (Google Remote Procedure Call) is a high-performance, open-source RPC framework developed by Google. It enables efficient communication between services in microservices architectures by using Protocol Buffers (protobufs) for serialization and HTTP/2 as the transport protocol. Here’s a detailed breakdown of gRPC:

gRPC is a modern, high-performance framework for inter-service communication, supporting multiple programming languages. It is commonly used in to enable fast and efficient communication between backend services.

As applications grow in complexity — especially in microservices architectures — efficient communication between services becomes critical. Traditional REST-based APIs, while widely used, have performance and scalability limitations. gRPC solves these issues by offering faster, more efficient, and scalable communication

Uses HTTP/2 Instead of HTTP/1.1

  • Multiple requests can be sent in parallel over a single connection, reducing overhead.
  • Supports , where both client and server can send messages independently.
  • Reduces network bandwidth consumption.

Protocol Buffers (Protobuf) Instead of JSON

  • Binary serialization is more compact and faster than JSON.
  • Prevents data structure mismatches between client and server.
  • Backward and forward compatibility: Older clients can communicate with newer servers and vice versa.

Supports Multiple Languages

  • Official support for Go, Python, Java, Node.js, C++, C#, Ruby, PHP, etc.
  • Makes it easier to build (multi-language) microservices.

Authentication and Security

  • Supports (Transport Layer Security) for encryption.
  • Can integrate with OAuth, JWT, and API Gateway security models.

You define and using Protocol Buffers.

Generate Code for Client & Server

The .proto file is compiled using the protoc compiler to generate for the target language.

The server implements the defined RPC methods.

The client uses the generated stub to make remote procedure calls.

Unary RPC (Simple Request-Response)

  • Client sends a request → Server processes it → Server sends back a response.
  • Client sends a request → Server streams multiple responses back.
  • Client sends multiple requests → Server processes and sends a single response.

Bidirectional Streaming RPC

  • Both client and server send and receive multiple messages asynchronously.

✅ Microservices communication✅ High-performance APIs with low latency✅ Streaming data (video, chat, real-time updates)✅ Language-agnostic distributed systems✅ IoT and mobile apps (efficient data transfer)

❌ Many browsers don’t support gRPC natively (use REST or GraphQL instead).❌ Client-side apps that require direct browser support: gRPC-Web is needed for browser support.

Best Practices for gRPC in Kubernetes

  1. : Prefer Envoy or Nginx over default Kubernetes proxies
  2. Implement Client-Side Load Balancing: Distribute connection logic
  3. Leverage Service Mesh Technologies: Istio, Linkerd
  4. : Continuous performance evaluation
  5. Consider Alternative Deployment Strategies: StatefulSets, headless services

As cloud-native architectures continue to evolve, gRPC is positioning itself as a critical technology for building interconnected, efficient, and flexible distributed systems. With growing support from major tech companies and an active open-source community, gRPC is more than just a communication protocol — it’s a strategic approach to designing robust, performant microservices.

In the world of distributed computing, gRPC isn’t just about sending data — it’s about sending data , , and .

Whether you’re building the next generation of cloud services, IoT platforms, or complex distributed systems, gRPC offers a powerful toolkit for creating communication layers that are as dynamic and responsive as the applications they support.

I’ll be diving deep into and why Kubernetes isn’t always the best fit for gRPC — along with how to ! Stay tuned for the full blog.

items.title: Kubernetes Cluster Creation using Ansible & Terraform: User data Bastion
items.author: Manohar Shetty
items.content:
Setting up a Kubernetes cluster manually can be time-consuming and error-prone, especially when dealing with multiple instances, SSH key management, and configuration updates. Automating this process with Bash and Ansible simplifies infrastructure provisioning, ensures consistency, and reduces deployment time.

In this blog, we will walk through a comprehensive automation script that:✅ Creates a dedicated user for automation

✅ Manages SSH keys and securely distributes them

✅ Installs AWS CLI and Ansible for infrastructure management

✅ Fetches instance IPs dynamically from AWS

✅ Updates Ansible inventory files and configuration settings

By the end of this guide, you will have a fully automated workflow to set up and manage your Kubernetes cluster effortlessly.

You can see the implementation video here

The script automates the following steps:

Creates a new user (ansible-user)..Grants sudo privileges without a password.

. Switches to the newly created user.

2. Installs and .Generates an SSH key if it does not exist.. Uploads the public key to an S3 bucket.

. Fetches and configures the SSH key from S3.

4. Ansible Playbook Cloning. Clones the ansible_playbook_k8s-installation repository.5. Updating Ansible Inventory. Dynamically fetches AWS EC2 instance IPs (for NFS, Bastion, Master, and Worker nodes).. Updates the inventory file (inventory.ini) accordingly.

. Introduces delays to ensure instances are up before fetching IPs.

6. Configuring Kubernetes Setup Files1. Updates and in main.yaml.2. Updates in nfs-setup/defaults/main.yaml.

This ensures administrative privileges for executing the script.

user_name="ansible-user"user_home="/home/$user_name"
user_ssh_dir="$user_home/.ssh"

3. Check if the User ExistsThis prevents duplicate user creation.

if id "$user_name" &>/dev/null; then  echo "User $user_name already exists."fi

Creates ansible-user without a password.

sudo adduser --disabled-password --gecos "" "$user_name"echo "User $user_name is created successfully"

Allows the user to run commands without a password.

echo "ansible-user ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL" | sudo tee /etc/sudoers.d/ansible-user

7. Install AWS CLI and Ansible

sudo apt update -ysudo apt-get install -y awscli
sudo apt-add-repository ppa:ansible/ansible -y
sudo apt update -y
sudo apt install ansible -y
mkdir -p $user_ssh_dirchmod 700 $user_ssh_dir
if [ ! -f "$user_ssh_dir/id_rsa" ]; then
  ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 -f $user_ssh_dir/id_rsa -N ""
chown -R $user_name:$user_name $user_home

9. Upload SSH Key to AWS S3

aws s3 cp $user_ssh_dir/id_rsa.pub s3://my-key/server.pub

10. Download and Apply SSH Key

aws s3 cp s3://my-key/server.pub $user_ssh_dir/authorized_keyschmod 600 $user_ssh_dir/authorized_keys
chown -R $user_name:$user_name $user_home

11. Clone the Ansible Playbook Repository

git clone "https://github.com/Manohar-1305/ansible_playbook_k8s-installation.git"

12. Define Inventory File and Logging

INVENTORY_FILE="ansible_playbook_k8s-installation/ansible/inventories/inventory.ini"LOG_FILE="ansible_script.log"
export AWS_REGION=ap-south-1
log() {  local message="$1"
  echo "$(date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") - $message" | sudo tee -a "$LOG_FILE"
}

14. Update Inventory Entries

update_entry() {  local section=$1  local ip=$3
  log "Updating entry: Section: $section, Host: $host, IP: $ip"
  if ! grep -q "^\[$section\]" "$INVENTORY_FILE"; then
    log "Section $section not found. Adding section header."
    sudo bash -c "echo -e '\n[$section]' >>'$INVENTORY_FILE'"
  fi
  sudo sed -i "/^\[$section\]/,/^\[.*\]/{/^$host ansible_host=.*/d}" "$INVENTORY_FILE"
  sudo sed -i "/^\[$section\]/a $host ansible_host=$ip" "$INVENTORY_FILE"

15. Fetch and Update NFS and Bastion IPs

NFS_IP=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region "$AWS_REGION" --filters "Name=tag:Name,Values=nfs" --query "Reservations[*].Instances[*].PrivateIpAddress" --output text)BASTION_IP=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region "$AWS_REGION" --filters "Name=tag:Name,Values=bastion" --query "Reservations[*].Instances[*].PublicIpAddress" --output text)
update_entry "local" "bastion" "$BASTION_IP"
update_entry "nfs-server" "nfs" "$NFS_IP"

16. Fetch and Update Master and Worker Node IPs

master=("master1" "master2" "master3")worker=("worker1" "worker2" "worker3")for master_node in "${master[@]}"; do
  ip=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region "$AWS_REGION" --filters "Name=tag:Name,Values=$master_node" --query "Reservations[*].Instances[*].PrivateIpAddress" --output text)
  if [ -n "$ip" ]; then
    master_ips["$master_node"]=$ip
  fifor worker_node in "${worker[@]}"; do
  ip=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region "$AWS_REGION" --filters "Name=tag:Name,Values=$worker_node" --query "Reservations[*].Instances[*].PrivateIpAddress" --output text)
  if [ -n "$ip" ]; then
    worker_ips["$worker_node"]=$ip
  fi

17. Update Inventory with Master and Worker Nodes

update_entry "controlplane" "master3" "${master_ips[master3]}"update_entry "controlplane" "master2" "${master_ips[master2]}"
update_entry "controlplane" "master1" "${master_ips[master1]}"
update_entry "node" "worker3" "${worker_ips[worker3]}"
update_entry "node" "worker2" "${worker_ips[worker2]}"
update_entry "node" "worker1" "${worker_ips[worker1]}"

18. Update Load Balancer and Advertise Address

LOAD_BALANCER_IP=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region "$AWS_REGION" --filters "Name=tag:Name,Values=master1" --query "Reservations[*].Instances[*].PrivateIpAddress" --output text)advertise_address=$LOAD_BALANCER_IP
FILE_PATH="/home/ansible-user/ansible_playbook_k8s-installation/ansible/roles/first-master/defaults/main.yaml"
if [ -f "$FILE_PATH" ]; then
  sudo sed -i.bak "s/^LOAD_BALANCER_IP:.*/LOAD_BALANCER_IP: ${LOAD_BALANCER_IP}/" "$FILE_PATH"
  sudo sed -i.bak "s/^advertise_address:.*/advertise_address: ${advertise_address}/" "$FILE_PATH"
fi

19. Update NFS IP in Configuration

NFS_IP=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region "$AWS_REGION" --filters "Name=tag:Name,Values=nfs" --query "Reservations[*].Instances[*].PrivateIpAddress" --output text)YAML_FILE="/home/ansible-user/ansible_playbook_k8s-installation/ansible/roles/nfs-setup/defaults/main.yaml"
if [[ -n "$NFS_IP" ]]; then
  sudo sed -i "s/^nfs_ip:.*/nfs_ip: \"$NFS_IP\"/" "$YAML_FILE"
  echo "Updated nfs_ip in $YAML_FILE to $NFS_IP"
else
  echo "Failed to fetch NFS IP. Please check the AWS command or instance state."
  exit 1

This script automates Kubernetes infrastructure setup by creating a user, managing SSH keys, installing necessary tools, fetching AWS instance IPs, and updating Ansible inventory files. It significantly reduces manual intervention and ensures a consistent deployment process.

items.title: Data Pipeline Observability: Monitoring, Logging, and Alerting using OpenTelemetry, Prometheus…
items.author: Yousef Yousefi
items.content:
Data Pipeline Observability: Monitoring, Logging, and Alerting using OpenTelemetry, Prometheus, Grafana, and Great Expectations

In modern , pipelines are no longer just about moving data — they must be observable, resilient, and self-healing. Without proper observability, data teams are blind to failures, latency spikes, and data quality issues.

This article will cover advanced techniques for monitoring and debugging data pipelines, including:✅ Real-time monitoring with OpenTelemetry & Prometheus✅ Automated data quality checks using Great Expectations✅ Distributed logging for big data workloads✅ Anomaly detection and alerting with Grafana & PagerDuty

By the end, you’ll have a production-ready observability stack for ensuring across your data infrastructure.

Why Observability Matters in Data Pipelines

A well-monitored data pipeline can:✅ Detect failures before they impact end users✅ Track data quality anomalies (missing or corrupt data)✅ Alert teams in real time about performance degradation✅ Provide historical logs for root cause analysis

Key Components of Data Observability

  1. Collect real-time metrics (latency, throughput, success/failure rates).
  2. Capture structured logs for debugging job failures.
  3. Notify teams when data issues arise (e.g., missing data).
  4. Track end-to-end execution of pipeline tasks.

Implementing Advanced Monitoring in Data Pipelines

1️⃣ Real-Time Monitoring with Prometheus & OpenTelemetry

To monitor data pipelines, we need real-time metrics collection. is the for instrumenting pipelines, and is used for storing & querying these metrics.

Instrument a Spark Job with OpenTelemetry Metrics

from opentelemetry import metricsfrom opentelemetry.sdk.metrics import MeterProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics.export import PrometheusMetricReader
from prometheus_client import start_http_server
# Start Prometheus exporter
reader = PrometheusMetricReader()
metrics.set_meter_provider(MeterProvider(metric_readers=[reader]))
meter = metrics.get_meter("spark-job")records_processed = meter.create_counter(    description="Number of records processed in Spark",
# Start Prometheus endpoint
start_http_server(8000)
# Simulating metric updates
def process_data(batch):
    records_processed.add(len(batch))

This exposes real-time metrics to Prometheus.

2️⃣ Configuring Prometheus to Collect Pipeline Metrics

Add the following to :

filebeat.inputs:  - type: log      - "/var/log/spark-pipeline.log"  hosts: ["http://elasticsearch:9200"]

This makes Spark’s metrics available for monitoring.

3️⃣ Distributed Logging for Data Pipelines

Logs are critical for in distributed pipelines. Instead of using raw log files, a structured logging framework like ELK (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) helps aggregate logs across systems.

Enable Structured Logging in Spark

from pyspark.sql import SparkSessionimport loggingspark = SparkSession.builder.appName("ObservabilityDemo").getOrCreate()
# Configure structured logging
logger = logging.getLogger("spark-pipeline")
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logger.info("Starting Spark job...")
df = spark.read.json("s3://data-source/")
logger.info(f"Read {df.count()} records from S3.")
df.write.parquet("s3://processed-data/")
logger.info("Data processing completed successfully.")

This logs structured messages that can be collected by Elasticsearch.

4️⃣ Streaming Logs to Elasticsearch using Filebeat

1. Install to forward logs to Elasticsearch:

sudo apt install filebeat

2. Configure :

filebeat.inputs:  - type: log      - "/var/log/spark-pipeline.log"  hosts: ["http://elasticsearch:9200"]

Now, all Spark logs are searchable in Kibana.

Automating Data Quality Checks with Great Expectations

Bad data can cause silent failures. ensures that data meets predefined quality constraints before it moves downstream.

Install Great Expectations

pip install great_expectations

Define Data Quality Constraints

import great_expectations as gx
context = gx.get_context()
# Create expectation suite
suite = context.create_expectation_suite("data_quality_checks")batch = context.get_batch("s3://processed-data/")
batch.expect_column_values_to_not_be_null("user_id")
batch.expect_column_values_to_be_between("transaction_amount", min_value=1, max_value=10000)results = batch.validate()
if not results["success"]:
    raise ValueError("Data quality check failed!")

Now, data pipelines will fail fast if data doesn’t meet quality standards.

6️⃣ Setting Up Alerts for Failures & Anomalies

Send Alerts to PagerDuty When Data Fails Validation

import requests
PAGERDUTY_API_KEY = "your_api_key"
def send_alert(error_message):
    payload = {
        "routing_key": PAGERDUTY_API_KEY,
        "event_action": "trigger",            "summary": error_message,            "source": "data-pipeline"    }
    requests.post("https://events.pagerduty.com/v2/enqueue", json=payload)

Now, if a data pipeline fails, PagerDuty will alert the team.

7️⃣ Visualizing Pipeline Health in Grafana

Grafana is used to visualize real-time metrics from Prometheus.

  1. Configure Prometheus as a data source in Grafana.
  2. Create to track:

    This guide covered end-to-end observability for data pipelines, including:✅ Real-time monitoring with Prometheus & OpenTelemetry✅ Structured logging with Elasticsearch & Filebeat✅ Data quality validation using Great Expectations✅ Automated alerting with PagerDuty✅ Visualization with Grafana

items.title: Mastering Dependency Injection in Spring Boot Microservices
items.author: Pradeep K
items.content:
Dependency Injection (DI) is a fundamental concept in Spring Boot that enables better code modularity, testability, and maintainability. In a microservices architecture, mastering DI ensures efficient service communication, reduces coupling, and enhances scalability.

What is Dependency Injection?

Dependency Injection is a design pattern that allows a class to receive its dependencies from an external source rather than creating them itself. In Spring Boot, DI is managed using the Spring IoC (Inversion of Control) container, which injects dependencies at runtime.

Types of Dependency Injection in Spring Boot

Spring Boot supports three types of Dependency Injection:

  1. (Preferred method)

Constructor injection is recommended as it ensures and are always initialized.

@Servicepublic class OrderService {
    private final PaymentService paymentService;
    public OrderService(PaymentService paymentService) {
        this.paymentService = paymentService;
    }
    public void processOrder() {
        paymentService.processPayment();}

Field injection is simpler but as it makes unit testing harder.

@Servicepublic class OrderService {    private PaymentService paymentService;
    public void processOrder() {
        paymentService.processPayment();}

Setter injection allows optional dependencies and flexibility but can lead to inconsistent state if not handled properly.

@Servicepublic class OrderService {
    private PaymentService paymentService;
    public void setPaymentService(PaymentService paymentService) {
        this.paymentService = paymentService;
    }
    public void processOrder() {
        paymentService.processPayment();}

Using @Component, @Service, and @Repository

Spring Boot uses annotations to automatically manage dependencies:

  •  — Generic component for DI.
  •  — Used for service layer logic.
  •  — Used for data access layer, integrating with databases.
@Componentpublic class NotificationService {
    public void sendNotification() {
        System.out.println("Sending notification...");}

Configuring Dependency Injection with @Bean

For more control, you can define beans in a configuration class:

@Configurationpublic class AppConfig {    public PaymentService paymentService() {
        return new PaymentService();
    }

Managing Dependencies in Microservices

In a microservices architecture, services communicate using REST, messaging, or service discovery. Dependency injection helps manage inter-service dependencies efficiently.

Example: Injecting a Feign Client for Microservices Communication

Spring Cloud Feign simplifies service-to-service communication.

@FeignClient(name = "payment-service")public interface PaymentClient {
    @GetMapping("/payment/process")
    String processPayment();

Injecting the Feign client:

@Servicepublic class OrderService {
    private final PaymentClient paymentClient;
    public OrderService(PaymentClient paymentClient) {
        this.paymentClient = paymentClient;
    }
    public void processOrder() {
        System.out.println(paymentClient.processPayment());}

Mastering Dependency Injection in Spring Boot microservices ensures efficient code structure, reduces coupling, and enhances maintainability. By leveraging constructor injection, @Component-based beans, and externalized configurations, you can build scalable and modular microservices with ease.

As we dive into 2025, we’re thrilled to announce our latest group of AWS Heroes! These exceptional individuals have demonstrated outstanding expertise and innovation, and are committed to sharing knowledge. Their contributions to the AWS community are greatly appreciated, and today we’re excited to celebrate them.

Ahmed Bebars – New Jersey, USA

Container Hero Ahmed Bebars is a Principal Engineer at The New York Times, where he leads the design and delivery of scalable developer platforms that enhance productivity and empower engineering teams. With deep expertise in containers, Kubernetes, and cloud-native technologies, Ahmed builds robust infrastructure solutions that streamline development workflows and support innovation. As an active member of the AWS and Cloud Native communities, he frequently shares his expertise at events like KubeCon, AWS re:Invent, AWS Community Days, and Kubernetes Community Days. Through his initiative, “Level Up with Ahmed,” he shares practical insights and resources to help engineers grow their skills and stay ahead in the evolving tech landscape.

Badri Narayanan Kesavan – Singapore

Community Hero Badri Narayanan Kesavan is an Engineering Lead and Solutions Architect with over a decade of professional experience, specializing in AWS Cloud solutions, platform engineering, and DevOps automation. Badri’s passion lies in learning and sharing with the community. He has delivered several talks at AWS Singapore Summit, AWS Summit ASEAN 2023, AWS Community Days, and various user group meetups. As an active AWS community leader, he organizes meetups and workshops under the AWS Singapore User Group where he regularly conducts sessions on diverse AWS topics, engaging a wide audience to foster innovation and collaboration. He has also authored the book “Mastering Amazon EC2)” which is a comprehensive guide to building robust and resilient applications on Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2).

Marcelo Paiva – Goiânia, Brazil

Community Hero Marcelo Paiva has over 30 years of experience in technology and is the CTO at Softprime Soluções, leading product development and research in digital biometrics, facial recognition, and artificial intelligence (AI). With more than a decade working with cloud computing, he specializes in building scalable and innovative solutions using AWS. Passionate about tech communities, Marcelo founded the AWS User Group in Goiânia in 2018, helping grow the local community. Today, he organizes events such as JoinCommunity and Cloud Summit Cerrado, fostering learning and networking in the Cerrado region.

Raphael Jambalos – Quezon City, Philippines

Community Hero Raphael Jambalos manages the Cloud Native development team at eCloudValley Philippines. His team has implemented dozens of cloud-native applications across multiple industries for customers in the Philippines. He is active in helping the AWS User Groups grow in the Philippines, and is passionate about connecting with the community beyond Manila. In his free time, he loves to read books and write about cloud technologies on his Dev.to blog.

Stav Ochakovski – Tel Aviv, Israel

Container Hero Stav Ochakovski is a DevOps Engineer at Beacon and a cybersecurity expert, managing highly scalable multi-cloud environments. With a background in DevOps engineering and instruction, Stav seamlessly transitioned into the dynamic cybersecurity start-up scene. A champion of container technologies and Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (Amazon EKS), she brings deep expertise in Kubernetes, CI/CD pipelines, and logging solutions to the AWS community. Stav actively shares her expertise by speaking at AWS community events, as well as security and container conferences. She is also a leader of the Israel AWS User Group and is a pastry-chef school graduate and licensed skipper.

Visit the AWS Heroes website if you’d like to learn more about the AWS Heroes program, or to connect with a Hero near you.

items.title: Quorum Quest: A Lightweight Distributed Leader Election Implementation
items.author: Aviv Laufer
items.content:
In distributed systems, coordination is everything. When multiple nodes need to work together seamlessly, one of the fundamental challenges is determining which node should lead. This is where leader election algorithms come into play — and it’s the problem I set out to solve with Quorum Quest.

Understanding Leader Election

Before diving into my implementation, let’s take a step back to understand what leader election is and why it matters.

In distributed systems, leader election is the process by which nodes in a cluster identify a single node to act as a coordinator or “leader” for specific operations. The leader typically takes on responsibilities like:

  • Coordinating write operations to ensure consistency
  • Distributing work among followers
  • Making critical decisions when consensus is required
  • Serving as the primary point of contact for client applications

Without a proper leader election mechanism, distributed systems risk facing split-brain conditions (where multiple nodes believe they’re the leader), deadlocks, or inconsistent states.

Traditional leader election algorithms include Paxos, Raft, and Zab (used in ZooKeeper). These algorithms are powerful but can be complex to implement and may feel like overkill for simpler applications. This is the gap Quorum Quest aims to fill.

Quorum Quest is a lightweight distributed leader election library I’ve developed in Go. Its main goal is to provide a simple, reliable way to elect a leader among a group of nodes without the complexity of implementing a full consensus algorithm.

The project follows a philosophy I’ve maintained in my other open-source work: simplicity without sacrificing reliability. By focusing on the core leader election problem and leaving advanced consensus features to specialized libraries, Quorum Quest offers a pragmatic approach for many real-world scenarios.

The design of Quorum Quest focuses on flexibility, reliability, and operational simplicity. The architecture consists of several key components:

Quorum Quest is structured as a gRPC service with client libraries, making it easy to integrate with applications in different languages. The core components include:

  1. : Provides a consistent API for leadership operations
  2. : Pluggable storage implementations for different environments
  3. : Language-specific clients that handle communication with the server
  4. : Built-in metrics and logging for operational visibility

The system follows a clean architecture pattern with clear separation between the service layer, storage layer, and client implementations.

Quorum Quest provides flexibility through support for multiple storage backends:

  • : For high-throughput, low-latency distributed deployments
  • : For simpler deployments leveraging existing Redis infrastructure
  • : For AWS-native applications and serverless architectures

The system detects the backend type at startup:

// Detect backend type from configbackendType, err := config.DetectBackendType(*configPath)    log.Fatalf("Failed to detect backend type: %v", err)// Initialize the appropriate store based on backend typecase "scylladb":
    // Initialize ScyllaDB store
case "dynamodb":
    // Initialize DynamoDB store
case "redis":
    // Initialize Redis store
default:
    log.Fatalf("Unsupported backend type: %s", backendType)
}

The library implements the same leadership semantics across all backends, ensuring consistent behavior regardless of which storage solution you choose. This allows you to select the backend that best fits your existing infrastructure without changing your application code.

Event-Driven Architecture

Quorum Quest implements an event-driven architecture to simplify leadership management in distributed applications. This approach allows your application to focus on to do when leadership changes, rather than the election algorithm works:

// Implement the Callbacks interface for your applicationtype MyCallbacks struct {
    // Your application state here
}
// Called when leadership status changes
func (c *MyCallbacks) OnLeaderElected(isLeader bool) {        // This node is now the leader    } else {
        // Another node is the leader
        runFollowerMode()}
// Called when this node was leader but lost leadership
func (c *MyCallbacks) OnLeaderLost() {
    // Stop leader-specific activities
    stopLeaderTasks()// Register callbacks with the client
client, err := quorumquestclient.NewQuorumQuestClient(
    "my-service",    15*time.Second,    quorumquestclient.WithCallbacks(callbacks),

This clean separation of concerns isolates election mechanics from business logic, allowing developers to focus entirely on application-specific behavior rather than distributed coordination details.

Graceful Failure Handling

Distributed systems must be resilient to failures. Quorum Quest incorporates:

  • Heartbeat mechanisms to detect node failures
  • Automatic re-election when a leader becomes unavailable
  • Connection retry policies for temporary backend unavailability
  • Clean shutdown processes that properly surrender leadership

Quorum Quest uses a flexible configuration system that supports multiple backends through a simple YAML file:

serverAddress: "localhost:5050"store:  port: 9042  table: "services"  consistency: "CONSISTENCY_QUORUM"    - "localhost:9042"  level: "LOG_LEVELS_INFOLEVEL"  serviceName: "quorum-quest"  environment: "development"
  otelEndpoint: "localhost:4317"
backend:
  type: "scylladb"  # Can be "scylladb", "redis", or "dynamodb"

The system automatically detects the backend type and loads the appropriate configuration schema, making it simple to switch between storage options without changing your code. Each backend has its own configuration section with appropriate parameters, while sharing common settings for observability and server configuration.

Use Cases and Applications

Quorum Quest shines in several scenarios:

  1. Scheduled job coordination: Ensuring cron-like jobs run on exactly one node
  2. : Electing a coordinator for work distribution
  3. : Having a single node responsible for configuration updates
  4. : Running services that shouldn’t be duplicated in a cluster

For example, in a microservices environment with multiple instances of the same service, you might want only one instance to process certain types of events or perform system-wide operations.

Performance Considerations

While simplicity is a focus, performance still matters. Quorum Quest is designed to be lightweight with:

  • Minimal ScyllaDB operations per election cycle
  • Low CPU and memory overhead
  • Configurable heartbeat intervals to balance responsiveness with resource usage

For most applications with a reasonable number of nodes (tens or hundreds), the performance impact is negligible.

If you’re interested in using Quorum Quest, the process is straightforward:

  1. Install the library: go get github.com/avivl/quorum-quest
  2. Create a configuration based on your ScyllaDB cluster
  3. Implement the callback interface for your application
  4. Start the election process and handle leadership appropriately

The GitHub repository includes examples and more detailed documentation to help you get started.

Distributed leader election doesn’t have to be complicated. With Quorum Quest, I’ve aimed to provide a solution that’s both easy to use and reliable enough for production environments.

By leveraging ScyllaDB’s consistency guarantees and a clean API, the library enables teams to implement leader election without becoming experts in consensus algorithms.

I welcome contributions and feedback on the project. If you’re building distributed systems in Go and need a straightforward leader election mechanism, give Quorum Quest a try!

What distributed system challenges are you facing in your projects? Have you implemented leader election before? Share your experiences in the comments below.

items.title: Implementing a Jenkins CI/CD Pipeline with Docker, SonarQube, Trivy, and Slack
items.author: Sai Charan
items.content:
Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipelines help automate software development workflows, making them efficient and reliable. In this article, we will walk through a Jenkins pipeline that pulls source code from GitHub, scans it using SonarQube, builds a Docker image, scans it with Trivy, pushes it to DockerHub, and deploys it using Docker Compose. Additionally, we will integrate Slack notifications to keep teams updated on the pipeline status.

# JAVA INSTALLATIONsudo apt update
sudo apt install default-jdk -y
java -version
sudo wget -O /usr/share/keyrings/jenkins-keyring.asc \
  https://pkg.jenkins.io/debian-stable/jenkins.io-2023.key
echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/jenkins-keyring.asc]" \
  https://pkg.jenkins.io/debian-stable binary/ | sudo tee \
  /etc/apt/sources.list.d/jenkins.list > /dev/null
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install jenkins

3. Install Python Sonar Scanner

# Install python3-venv package if not already installedsudo apt install python3.12-venv
# Create a new virtual environment
python3 -m venv /home/ubuntu/myenv
# Activate the virtual environment
source /home/ubuntu/myenv/bin/activate
# Install the required packages
pip install pysonar-scanner
sudo apt updatesudo apt install -y wget apt-transport-https gnupg lsb-release
wget -qO - https://aquasecurity.github.io/trivy-repo/deb/public.key | sudo apt-key add -
echo "deb https://aquasecurity.github.io/trivy-repo/deb $(lsb_release -cs) main" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/trivy.listsudo apt install -y trivy

5. Install Docker Compose

# Download the Docker Compose binarysudo curl -L "https://github.com/docker/compose/releases/download/1.29.2/docker-compose-$(uname -s)-$(uname -m)" -o /usr/local/bin/docker-compose
# Set permissions to make the binary executable
sudo chmod +x /usr/local/bin/docker-compose
# Check Docker Compose version
docker-compose --version

6. Slack integration with jenkins pipeline you can follow this link

Setting Up Jenkins Master-Agent Architecture

1. Generate SSH Keys on the Master Node

ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 -C "jenkins-master"

This will generate a public (id_rsa.pub) and private (id_rsa) key pair.

2. Copy the Master Node Public Key to the Worker Node

ssh-copy-id -i ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub ubuntu@WORKER_NODE_IP

Alternatively, manually add the contents of id_rsa.pub to the worker node's ~/.ssh/authorized_keys file.

3. Add Private Key to Jenkins Credentials

  1. Go to Jenkins Dashboard > Manage Jenkins > Manage Credentials.
  2. Add a new SSH Username with Private Key credential.
  3. Set as JENKINS_SSH_KEY, as ubuntu, and paste the contents of id_rsa in the private key field.

4. Create a New Node in Jenkins

  1. Navigate to Manage Jenkins > Manage Nodes and Clouds.
  2. Click , enter a name (e.g., WORKER-NODE), and select .
  3. Set to /home/ubuntu/jenkins.
  4. Assign : WORKER-NODE.
  5. Enter the worker node IP and select JENKINS_SSH_KEY from credentials.
  6. Choose : .
  7. Host Key Verification Strategy → Non verifying Verification Strategy
  • Slack Notification Plugin
  • SONAR_AUTH_TOKEN (SonarQube authentication token)
  • DOCKERHUB_CRED (DockerHub username and password)
  • SLACK_SECRET (Slack authentication token)

Jenkins Declarative Pipeline

Below is the Jenkins declarative pipeline script

pipeline {    agent { label 'WORKER-NODE' }        GIT_REPO = 'https://github.com/${YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME}/${YOUR_REPO}.git'
        IMAGE_NAME = '${DOCKER_USERNAME}/${YOUR_IMAGE_NAME}'
        IMAGE_TAG = 'latest'
        SLACK_CHANNEL = '#your-slack-channel'
        SLACK_CREDENTIAL_ID = 'SLACK_SECRET'
        SONAR_HOST_URL = 'http://your-sonarqube-server:9000'
        SONAR_PROJECT_KEY = 'your-sonar-project-key'
        SONAR_CREDENTIAL_ID = 'SONAR_AUTH_TOKEN'
        DOCKER_CREDENTIAL_ID = 'DOCKERHUB_CRED'        stage('Checkout') {                echo "Cloning repository from ${env.GIT_REPO}"
                git branch: 'main', url: env.GIT_REPO
                script { slackNotification('Checkout') }        }
        stage('Source Code Scanning') {
            steps {
                echo "Running SonarQube scan"
                withCredentials([string(credentialsId: env.SONAR_CREDENTIAL_ID, variable: 'AUTH_TOKEN')]) {                        /home/ubuntu/myenv/bin/pysonar-scanner \
                        -Dsonar.projectKey=${env.SONAR_PROJECT_KEY} \
                        -Dsonar.sources=. \
                        -Dsonar.host.url=${env.SONAR_HOST_URL} \
                        -Dsonar.login=$AUTH_TOKEN                }
                script { slackNotification('Source Code Scanning') }
            }            steps {
                echo "Building Docker image: ${env.IMAGE_NAME}:${env.IMAGE_TAG}"
                sh "docker build -t ${env.IMAGE_NAME}:${env.IMAGE_TAG} ."
                script { slackNotification('Build') }
            }
        stage('Scanning Container Image') {
            steps {
                echo "Scanning Docker image with Trivy"
                sh "trivy image --format table ${env.IMAGE_NAME}:${env.IMAGE_TAG}"
                script { slackNotification('Scanning Container Image') }
            }
        stage('DockerHub Login') {
            steps {
                echo "Logging in to DockerHub"
                withCredentials([usernamePassword(credentialsId: env.DOCKER_CREDENTIAL_ID, passwordVariable: 'DOCKER_PASSWORD', usernameVariable: 'DOCKER_USERNAME')]) {
                    sh "echo $DOCKER_PASSWORD | docker login -u $DOCKER_USERNAME --password-stdin"
                }
                script { slackNotification('DockerHub Login') }
            }
        stage('Docker Tag & Push') {
            steps {
                echo "Tagging and pushing Docker image"
                sh "docker tag ${env.IMAGE_NAME}:${env.IMAGE_TAG} ${env.IMAGE_NAME}:latest"
                sh "docker push ${env.IMAGE_NAME}:latest"
                script { slackNotification('Docker Tag & Push') }        }            steps {
                echo "Deploying with Docker Compose"
                sh "docker-compose up -d --build"
                script { slackNotification('Deploy') }
            }    }
def slackNotification(stageName) {
    slackSend(
        channel: env.SLACK_CHANNEL, 
        message: "Stage '${stageName}' completed", 
        tokenCredentialId: env.SLACK_CREDENTIAL_ID,
        username: 'bot'}

Alternatively, you can refer to my GitHub repository for the jenkins file.

Alternatively, you can refer to my GitHub repository for the Source Code of Django application.

Breakdown of the Pipeline

The pipeline starts by pulling the latest code from GitHub.

2. Source Code Analysis with SonarQube

This stage scans the source code using SonarQube for static code analysis.

3. Building a Docker Image

The application is containerized by building a Docker image.

4. Scanning the Docker Image with Trivy

The built Docker image is scanned for vulnerabilities using Trivy.

5. Logging in to DockerHub

Jenkins authenticates with DockerHub using stored credentials.

6. Pushing the Image to DockerHub

The image is tagged and pushed to the DockerHub repository.

7. Deploying with Docker Compose

The application is deployed using docker-compose up to bring up the latest containerized version.

Each stage of the pipeline sends a Slack notification to keep teams updated about the pipeline’s progress and completion.

This Jenkins pipeline automates the entire CI/CD process from source code checkout to deployment. It ensures best practices by incorporating:

  • Static code analysis (SonarQube)
  • Security scanning (Trivy)
  • Automated container deployment (Docker Compose)
  • Real-time Slack notifications for better monitoring and collaboration.

items.title: Kokoro-82M: A Compact and Efficient Text-to-Speech Model
items.author: Carlos Biagolini-Jr.
items.content:
Text-to-speech (TTS) technology has become increasingly essential in applications ranging from accessibility solutions to virtual assistants and audiobook production. While many TTS models exist, finding a balance between efficiency and high-quality speech synthesis can be challenging. Enter , a compact yet powerful open-source TTS model that offers impressive performance with only .

The original project is hosted on , where you can explore more details and access the model directly: Kokoro-82M on Hugging Face.

Developed to generate natural-sounding speech efficiently, competes with significantly larger models, offering fast synthesis without compromising audio quality. The model supports American and British English, as well as other languages, including Japanese, Mandarin Chinese, Spanish, French, Hindi, Italian, and Brazilian Portuguese. It is continuously evolving to support more languages and voice styles.

Its and make it a flexible choice for developers looking to integrate TTS into their applications.

  • Lightweight and Efficient — Unlike bulky neural TTS models, Kokoro-82M strikes a balance between computational efficiency and realistic speech generation.
  • High-Quality Audio Output — Despite its compact size, the model delivers clear and natural-sounding speech.
  •  — Users can select from various voice packs to fit their project needs.
  •  — Free to use, modify, and integrate into commercial and personal projects.
  •  — Kokoro-82M is simple to set up, with no complex configuration required.

Kokoro-82M is an excellent choice for developers, researchers, and educators who need a robust TTS model without the excessive resource consumption of larger alternatives. Now, let’s dive into how you can set it up and start generating speech with it.

Step-by-Step Guide: Installing and Using Kokoro-82M for English TTS

In this tutorial, we will walk through the process of setting up on your machine and generating speech from text in . If you want to explore Portuguese or other languages, you can check out my GitHub repository: PythonTtsKokoro.

Step 1: Update Your System

Before installing any dependencies, update your system’s package repositories to ensure compatibility with the latest software versions:

sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get upgrade -y

Step 2: Install Essential Dependencies (Ubuntu & macOS)

Kokoro TTS relies on espeak-ng for text processing. Below are the installation steps for both and .

Step 2.1: Ubuntu Installation

To install espeak-ng on Ubuntu, run:

sudo apt-get install espeak-ng -y

Step 2.2: macOS Installation

To install espeak-ng on macOS, follow these steps:

Step 2.2.1: Install espeak-ng

Ensure Homebrew is installed, then use the following command to install espeak-ng:

Step 2.2.2: Configure Environment Variables

After installation, configure the environment variables so the system and Python can locate espeak-ng.

Append the following lines at the end of the file:

LIB_PATH=$(find /opt/homebrew/Cellar/espeak-ng -name "libespeak-ng.dylib" | head -n 1)if [[ -n "$LIB_PATH" ]]; then
    export PHONEMIZER_ESPEAK_LIBRARY="$LIB_PATH"
fi

Save and exit (CTRL + X, then Y, then ENTER).

This configuration ensures that the PHONEMIZER_ESPEAK_LIBRARY variable always points to the correct version of libespeak-ng.dylib, even if espeak-ng is updated via Homebrew.

Step 2.2.3: Apply Changes

After modifying .zshrc, apply the changes by running:

Step 3: Install Kokoro TTS and Required Libraries

Next, install Kokoro-82M along with its dependencies using Python’s package manager:

pip install kokoro soundfile

Step 4: Clone the Project Repository (Optional)

For an easy-to-use implementation, clone my repository:

git clone https://github.com/biagolini/PythonTtsKokoro.gitcd PythonTtsKokoro

Step 5: Set Up a Virtual Environment (Recommended)

Using a virtual environment helps keep dependencies isolated:

python3 -m venv venvsource venv/bin/activate  # On Windows, use venv\Scripts\activate

Step 6: Generate Speech from Text

Now, let’s create a Python script (en.py) to convert text into speech:

from kokoro import KPipelineimport soundfile as sf
# Define output directory
output_folder = 'output'
if not os.path.exists(output_folder):
    os.makedirs(output_folder)
# Initialize Kokoro TTS pipeline
pipeline = KPipeline(lang_code='a')  # 'a' for Englishtext = '''
Hello and welcome! In this tutorial, we will explore how to use Kokoro-82M!
'''generator = pipeline(    voice='af_heart',     split_pattern=r'\n+'for i, (gs, ps, audio) in enumerate(generator):
    sf.write(f'{output_folder}/en_{i}.wav', audio, 24000)  # 24kHz sample rate

Execute the script to generate speech from the provided text:

The generated speech files will be saved in the directory as en_0.wav and can be played using any standard audio player.

Step 8: Play the Generated Audio

Open the .wav file in your preferred media player.

Kokoro-82M is an efficient and powerful TTS model that provides high-quality speech synthesis without heavy resource requirements. Whether you’re building a virtual assistant, an audiobook generator, or an accessibility tool, this model is a fantastic solution for natural-sounding text-to-speech applications.

If you’re interested in generating speech in Portuguese or exploring additional configurations, check out my for more examples: PythonTtsKokoro. Feel free to fork the project, experiment with different voices, and contribute to the community!

If you found this guide helpful, stay connected for more insights on AI, cloud security, and AWS automation:

Happy building with 🚀
